ref: Esau found out that his father Isaac had blessed Jacob and had warned him not to marry any of the Canaanite women. He also learned that Jacob had been sent to find a wife in northern Syria
text: Eesayuu nìn ká yii Isaaki mēē ki bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon ki bí yii ú nīn cá Padan-Aram kí cáá unimpu niin, ní ki tīī-u mara yii ú taa cáá unimpu Kanaan yab sapɔmbi kansikin.
translated: Esau learned that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him to Padan-Aram to take a wife for himself, and that he had forbidden him from marrying a Canaanite woman.

ref: he looked out in a field and saw a well where shepherds took their sheep for water. Three flocks of sheep were lying around the well, which was covered with a large rock.
text: U kpēēyee, lɔkɔ ubɔ dɔ̄ akpaanni tipicaŋkpun tita dɔ̄ ki kɔ́ĺ. Niin ní bipikpaaliibi nìn lúù nnyim ki nyùntī ipii. Ditaŋkpakpaandi diba nìn pīī lɔkɔee nyɔkɔpu.
translated: In the morning, the woman went to the market to buy three oranges. The children were playing outside in the compound. The elders were sitting under the tree.

ref: Laban said, “You are my nephew, and you are like one of my own family.” Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel After Jacob had been there for a month,
text: Ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «Tiŋman, a baasii sá m-niliu ní kiŋkiŋ.» Jaakɔb nìn kāl̀ Laaban cee ki sāā uŋmal ubɔ. Jaakɔb cáá Leeya nì Raseel
translated: Laban said to Jacob: "Indeed, you are my bone and my flesh." Jacob stayed with Laban for a whole month. Jacob married Leah and Rachel

ref: Laban said to him, “You shouldn't have to work without pay, just because you are a relative of mine. What do you want me to give you?”
text: Dalbee ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «A nín sá m-niliuee puee, á nīn tūǹ ditundi míǹ faalaa ki tìì-maaa? An puee tùkù-mi kun a là ḿ dāá pà-see.»
translated: Laban said to Jacob: "Because you are my relative, should you work for me for nothing? Tell me what your wages should be."

ref: At the end of the week of celebration, Laban let Jacob marry Rachel, and he gave her his servant woman Bilhah. Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, but he had to work another seven years for Laban.
text: Ní Jaakɔb nì Raseel mun kpáfì. Jaakɔb nìn néeń Raseel ki jítiǹ Leeya, ní ki nìn gíti kāl̀ abin aluli ki túń ditundi ki tīī Laaban. Jaakɔb biyaamu bɔti
translated: Jacob and Rachel had a child. Jacob loved Rachel more than Leah, and he worked seven more years to pay the bride price to Laban. Jacob's family grew

ref: Leah gave birth to a son and named him Reuben. Then she said, “The Lord has taken away my sorrow. Now my husband will love me more than he does Rachel.”
text: Leeya nìn dūu cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki, ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Rubeen. Di sá yii un nìn lī yii: «Ti-Dindaan ká m-falaau, kɔŋkɔnnee m-cal làá nīn kíǹ ki néeń-mi.»
translated: Leah became pregnant and gave birth to a son, and she named him Reuben. She said: "The Lord has seen my suffering, now my husband will love me."

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Kujoou Jaakɔb nyánní kusaau ki kùnnée ní Leeya túŋkì-u nsanni ní ki bí-u yii: «Man cee ní a làá dòon̄ díǹ. Kí ŋmā kí ká-si á dòon̄ m-ceeyee, m-jipɔɔn mandragɔɔr biliŋ ní m dūu pàn̄ m-yɔnti.» Ní Jaakɔb lafun dōoǹ u-cee kunyeeu gbanti.
translated: So Jacob's wife Rachel became jealous of her sister Leah and said to her: "Give me some of your son's mandrakes, because I want to use them to help me have children." And Jacob slept with her that night.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Di sá yii kí wàatī ḿ bàńee, tin a nìn cááyee kaa nìn náaǹ báà fiii. Ama kí yòon̄ ŋwiin ŋun m-taal tāā a-dumpuee, a-wanti wíiǹ tikpil ní ti-Dindaan bīiǹ a-boon. Too, kɔŋkɔnnee ŋyunti ŋulɔŋu ní man làá gíti dūú tɔ̄ ditundi kí ká tin làá ŋmàntī ḿ kpèè m-dicaŋkuee?»
translated: When that time comes for me to go, will you cry for me or will you just let me go? But if you see my coffin being carried to the cemetery, with people following behind singing. Then, how will your heart feel when you see all this happening without you being able to stop my death?

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bátì isufi in bi yíì peepiliiyee nì amandi nì plataanee pambiyaamu nsɔmu, ki cùkū cùkū-mu ki māntìi tú ki dāa kátì taapu, ní ki pɔ́kɔtì ajan ŋin u cùkūee, ní ndɔsɔmuee kíǹ ki cáá mpim mpim.
translated: And Jacob took fresh rods of poplar and almond and plane trees, and peeled white streaks in them, exposing the white of the rods, and the rods which he had peeled he set before the flocks in the watering troughs where the flocks came to drink.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun ní tiwaŋkuti tin yáàyee yaa làá jɔ̄m̀ tɔbee, ní Jaakɔb ń yòon̄ní ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee kí ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee kí túĺ ti-nimbiin, ní tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee.
translated: When the time came for him to die, Jacob called his son Joseph and said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and promise that you will show me kindness and faithfulness. Do not bury me in Egypt,"

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun tiwaŋkuti bíi jɔ́ḿ tɔbee ní m ká kudamintiiu poon yii ijaa in bíi jɔ́ḿ isalee sá atan atan nì iluntoon iluntoon nì ŋmàtì ŋmati.
translated: The reason why I am telling you this story is so that you can understand that it is not good to lie and to steal and to be greedy.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Niinee ní Laaban yōoǹ u-nabiyaamu ní bi nín jì Jaakɔb boon. Bi cōom̀ iwiin iluli ní ki tin nín cúú-u Galaadi jool pu.
translated: Now Laban and his relatives and Jacob went. They met each other at the mountain in the land of Gilead.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Ama un cee ní a yaa ká a-waaŋee, bí kpɔ̀-u. Ti-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ di bí nee. Dāan̄ m-cee kí kpèè a yaa ká a-wanti tiba á yòon̄.» Jaakɔb kaa nìn nyí yii Raseel di yùn̄ awaa.
translated: But if you come to my relatives and they refuse you, then you will be free from my oath. Only do not take my son back there." So Jacob swore an oath to him about this matter.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Tiba di yaa cúú a-waŋkuluuku kubɔee, maa cāabìń-si diwaŋkpil, see ḿ nyàab̀ kí téeǹ kun an cúúyee yil. A-waŋkuti tin bi yù ŋwiimpuee nì tin bi yù kunyeeuee, a bàlifī-mi ní ḿ téeǹ-si.
translated: When you arrive at the chief's compound, don't just enter the courtyard, wait until someone comes to call you to enter. The chief's children and his wives are there, show them respect and greet them.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M pátì kí fōō tiŋan tin a ŋá-mi nee nì tibulcin tin a dàkā man a-tɔntɔnliu nee, kun puee ŋkpandɔ baba ní m nìn ŋúb́ ki dūtì Jɔɔdan buŋŋu nee. Ama díǹ pu nee, m yākatì m-nib nì m-waŋkuti akiti ali ní nee.
translated: I did not think that I would reach the land that the LORD my God was giving me and the cities that he had promised me, but today I have crossed the Jordan River. But now, I have divided my people and my possessions among them all.

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «M gbāam̀-si, tùkù-mi a-yindi.» Ní u bí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a là kí béé m-yindi?» Niin niin ní u bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon.
translated: And Jacob asked him, saying: "Tell me your name." And he said to him: "Why do you ask for my name?" Right there he blessed Jacob.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: And he asked them saying: "My brother Esau will meet you and he will ask you saying: <Whose servants are you and where are you going? Whose animals are these that are in front of you?>"

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: Jacob stayed in his father's land. He lived where God had brought his father to stay.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: And he told them this parable: "A certain man had two sons and he asked them saying: <What do you think? A man had two sons and he went to the first one and said to him:>"

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Ní unaaciinee bālifì-u yii: «Bi yíì-si yii ba ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Bi yíì-mi yii Jaakɔb ní.»
translated: He asked him saying: "What is your name?" And he told him saying: "My name is Jacob."

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Kutaa wúntì an dáá bɔ̀ǹee ní Laaban yíkì ki kpàl̄ u-bisalib nì u-naantiibi ki kāaǹ-bi, ki mēē ki bīiǹ bi-pu ní ki fātìi nín kùǹ u-dumpu. Jaakɔb bɔ́ntì kí tūŋkì Eesayuu
translated: When Jacob heard that Laban's sons were grumbling and complaining about him, and saw that Laban's attitude toward him had changed, he became afraid of Esau

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: Jacob was very afraid. He cried to God to help him as he had promised.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: U cáá u-yab kɔkɔ ki dūtì mbun ki kpáaǹ tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.
translated: He took the red cloth and wrapped it around the red bird's neck.

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Ní unaaciinee bí-u yii: «Baa làá ti yíiń-si Jaakɔb, bi làá nīn kíǹ ki yíì-si yii Isirayeel ní. Di sá yii si nì Unimbɔti mɔ̀n̄ ní si nì binib mun mɔ̀n̄, ní a pɔ́ɔkiǹ bi-kɔkɔ.»
translated: And he prophesied about him saying: "What was said about Jacob, and what was written about Israel. It will happen to God's people and to those people, when he gathers them together."

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Ní Eesayuu kíí-u yii: «M-nabiki, m cáá tigaajati tikpil, dū a-wanti kí kpāafì.»
translated: "Jesus said to him: 'My friend, I did not wrong you, didn't you agree with me for one denarius?'"

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Ní Jaakɔb jipɔmbee kɔ́ kitiŋin ki fúkúl̀ anikpilee wanti ní ki kóoǹ bi-wanti kɔkɔ, kun puee bi bíil̀ bi-niisal.
translated: Jacob's sons went to buy grain in Egypt because there was famine in their land, and they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Bi nìn cúú bi-piii nì bi-ŋoobui nì bi-naai nì bi-mammu nì tin kɔkɔ nìn pée bí kitiŋ poon nì tisatinee.
translated: They have their children and their grandchildren and their great-grandchildren and their great-great-grandchildren and all those who will come after them in the future.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí Simeeyɔn nì Leefii yii: «Ni kɔ́ǹ-mi tibɔtin! Ni náań kudɔ̀ŋu ki bíl̀ man nì kitiŋ nee nib kansikin ní nee, an dàkà yii Kanaan yab nì Peeriisi yab. Man nib kaa náaǹ. Bíǹ ŋūǹ kí kpāfì kí dāá jáǹ-mi ní kí kúntí m-dicaŋku kɔkɔ.»
translated: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi: "You have brought trouble upon me! You have made me odious among the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites and the Perizzites. My men are few. If they unite against me and attack me, I and my household will be destroyed."

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Eesayuu cáá Kanaan tiŋkin ní binimpoobi. Ada un nìn sá Eelɔn, ueetija bisalee nì Ooliibama un nìn sá Ana bisal nì Siibeeyɔn ueefija naantiiuee.
translated: Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan. He married Adah daughter of Elon, a Hittite woman, and Oholibamah daughter of Anah and granddaughter of Zibeon, a Hivite woman.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Míǹ pu ní Eesayuu, un bi gíti yii Eedoomee, cūtìi kóòń Seeyiir jool pu. Eesayuu naantiibi
translated: This is about Esau, who is also called Edom, in the hill country of Seir. These are Esau's descendants

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Diisan jipɔmbi di sá Usi nì Aran.
translated: Today we will go to Usi and Aran.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Ní uninjee bí-u yii: «Bi yíkì doo, di sá yaadalee m nìn gbìl̄ bi bíi lì yii: <Tí yīkìmaan doo kí nīn cá Dootan.>» Ní Jooseefi nín jì bi-boon Dootan ní ki tin ká-bi niin.
translated: And the man said to him: "They have left here, because I heard them saying: <Let us go to Dothan.>" So Joseph went after his brothers and found them in Dothan.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Boonee ní u gíti dámintì ní ki tí dūu máafì u-mambi yii: «M tí dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ. M dámintì yii ŋwìiń nì uŋmal nì iŋmalbijaa saalaa nì ubɔ dòoń ki gbàantí m-nimbiin.»
translated: One day he said to his wife that he was going to the farm and said: "I am going to work on the farm. I am going so that you and the child and the children stay well and that things will be good for my family."

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Ní u-baa bí-u yii: «Á cù kí kpèè yii a-mambi pɔ̄ɔ̄ɔɔ, bi-waŋkuti mun bí mana, kí gítiń kí tùkù-mi.» Eebrɔn tantakalin ní bi nìn bí ní Jooseefi baa tú-u ní u yíkìi búntì Siikeem tiŋkin u-mambi cee.
translated: They said to him: "We have come to look for our brothers, the sons of our father, who are tending the flocks." The Hebrews found them and they told Joseph's brothers that they had seen them going towards Shechem to look for their father's flocks.

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Bi nín ká-u u páaǹní ndan kaa láá bāań-bee, ní bi sòon̄ ki bɔ́ntì yii bí kpɔ̀-u.
translated: If you don't greet people when you meet them on the road, they will think you are proud.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee Madiyan yab bíǹ dūu bàn̄ Eejipiti ní ki mun nìkī Jooseefi ki nyáfiǹ Pootifaar, ujanjakpil un cí Faaroo cindee.
translated: The Midianite merchants sold him in Egypt to a man named Joseph to Potiphar, the captain of Pharaoh's guard.

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Ní Juuda bí Oonan yii: «Fōō a-maan nimpuu ukpiipu kii mara nín pée sá puee, kí màal̀ kí téeǹ u-yil u-dicaŋku ń taa kúntí.»
translated: Judah said to Onan: "Go in to your brother's wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her, and raise up offspring for your brother."

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: U-cal Pootifaar dāa bāańee ní u gíiǹ ki téeǹ míǹ mbaantiim yii: «Ueebruja uyumbu un a cáań-tee dɔ́miń kí dòon̄ nì mi.
translated: Potiphar's wife lied to her husband saying: "That Hebrew slave you brought to stay with us came to me."

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ní ujanjakpilee lēē Jooseefi ú nīn bí bi-cee ki tútúǹ-bi atuun. Bi nìn yúntì sarka poon ŋwaam. Jooseefi lēē bisarkadambi bili damintiiti taapu
translated: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him and hated him very much. They sold him to some traders. Joseph was taken by the traders to a distant land.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Ama m gbāam̀-si, bi yaa fātìi fōō-si ní a-pɔbil sɔ̄ŋkèe, a mun ń téetí kí ŋá-mi tiniti kí sòoǹ m-bɔti kí tùkù Faaroo kí nyāntí-mi sarka nee poon.
translated: But I will harden his heart, so that he will not let the people go, and I will multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt, and I will lay my hand upon Pharaoh and bring out my armies.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: U kpēēyee ní ki ká ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee nyáń ki kútì idifin in béeyee pu.
translated: They said that they would help the children so that they could learn how to read and write well.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Man nì u nìn dámintì kunyabaantiiu poon ní báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.
translated: If you don't respect your parents, then you will not have respect and honor.

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Niinee ní Faaroo tú bi yíiń Jooseefi. Ní bi nyántì-u kusarkadiin malaa, ní u kōol̀ ki kpántì u-wampeeŋkaati ní ki nín cá ubɔti nimbiin.
translated: Pharaoh's daughter found Joseph. She took him as her own child, and he grew up in the royal palace and became like a prince.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: M yúee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlin ki dɔ́miń ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.
translated: When you want to prepare cassava leaves, you pound them well, squeeze out the water and put them in a pot to cook on the fire.

ref: God looked at the light and saw that it was good. He separated light from darkness
text: Ní Unimbɔti ká yii ŋwalifi ŋān̄, ní u cúú ŋwalifi nì dibɔmbɔndi ki yākatì tɔbin.
translated: God did not create the world, and he left the world in the hands of the devil.

ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: Ní Unimbɔti yíiń kugbɔŋuee yii kutagbɔŋu. Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwinliliiti dal dee.
translated: God does not like pride or boasting. If you do that, you will fall into trouble, it is very bad.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the third day. The Fourth Day
text: Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwintataati dal dee.
translated: If you don't have money to pay the debt, they will seize your property.

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Ní Unimbɔti náań unil ki nántiǹ u-ba, u náań-u ki nántiǹ úǹ Unimbɔti u-ba ní. U nìn náań uninja nì unimpu ní.
translated: God did not create us to be far from him, he created us to be close to God. He wants to have fellowship and friendship with us.

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: Míǹ mbaantiim, m dū timool ki tīī tiwaŋkuti tin bí kitiŋ puee kɔkɔ nì inoon kɔkɔ nì tin kɔkɔ pée fù ti-biŋŋi pu, ki bí kitiŋ pu ki cáá kufoouee án nīn sá tì-jinti.» Ní an ŋá míǹ.
translated: My brothers, I have lived my life before God in all good conscience up to this day, and I continue to do so even now in this council." That is what he said.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Unimbɔti túń u-tuuŋŋi ki dóò, ní ŋwinluluútee, u kāl̀ ki ŋūŋfì atuun ŋin kɔkɔ u ŋée pu.
translated: When a person is going to die, even if he is strong, he becomes weak like a child.

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Kutagbɔŋu nì kitiŋ náantam nín kíĺ puee dee. Eedeen kpaabun bɔti Ŋyunti ŋun ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti nyántì kitiŋ nì kutagbɔŋuee,
translated: The elders and the youth are not in agreement. They say that God the Creator does not agree with the elders and the youth,

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,

text: Kujoou Jaakɔb nyánní kusaau ki kùnnée ní Leeya túŋkì-u nsanni ní ki bí-u yii: «Man cee ní a làá dòon̄ díǹ. Kí ŋmā kí ká-si á dòon̄ m-ceeyee, m-jipɔɔn mandragɔɔr biliŋ ní m dūu pàn̄ m-yɔnti.» Ní Jaakɔb lafun dōoǹ u-cee kunyeeu gbanti.
translated: When Jacob came from the field in the evening, Leah went out to meet him and said to him: "You must come in to me, for I have hired you with my son's mandrakes." So he lay with her that night.

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «Sii a-ba nyí m nín túń ditundi din ki tīī-see nì m nín ŋúb́ a-waŋkuti tì būtì miŋyee.
translated: Jacob's sons answered: "If we give our sister to a man who is not circumcised, that would be a disgrace to us.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Di sá yii kí wàatī ḿ bàńee, tin a nìn cááyee kaa nìn náaǹ báà fiii. Ama kí yòon̄ ŋwiin ŋun m-taal tāā a-dumpuee, a-wanti wíiǹ tikpil ní ti-Dindaan bīiǹ a-boon. Too, kɔŋkɔnnee ŋyunti ŋulɔŋu ní man làá gíti dūú tɔ̄ ditundi kí ká tin làá ŋmàntī ḿ kpèè m-dicaŋkuee?»
translated: When that time comes for me to go, will you cry for me and miss me very much? But if you see a star shining in the sky at night, a bright star twinkling with the Evening Star beside it. Then, won't the sound of laughter remind you of me so that you won't be sad in your heart?

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bátì isufi in bi yíì peepiliiyee nì amandi nì plataanee pambiyaamu nsɔmu, ki cùkū cùkū-mu ki māntìi tú ki dāa kátì taapu, ní ki pɔ́kɔtì ajan ŋin u cùkūee, ní ndɔsɔmuee kíǹ ki cáá mpim mpim.
translated: When Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt, he said to his sons, "Why do you just keep looking at each other?" and he said, "I have heard that there is grain in Egypt, so go down there and buy some for us."

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun ní tiwaŋkuti tin yáàyee yaa làá jɔ̄m̀ tɔbee, ní Jaakɔb ń yòon̄ní ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee kí ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee kí túĺ ti-nimbiin, ní tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee.
translated: When the time came for him to die, Jacob called his son Joseph and said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and promise that you will show me kindness and faithfulness. Do not bury me in Egypt,"

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: Ama tiwaŋkuti tin sá ŋkaŋkalimuee yaa bāańee, waa yōontí ndɔsɔmuee ti-nimbiin tí jɔ̄m̀ tɔb. Ŋkaŋkalimu gbantee, Laaban yati dee, ní úǹ yati sá tin yáàyee. Jaakɔb sáǹ Laaban
translated: But we have not found anything that belongs to you, because you searched through all our things in front of everyone. Your relatives and mine are here as witnesses. Laban did not find anything that belonged to him. Jacob told Laban

translated: And Jacob took fresh rods of poplar and almond and plane trees, and peeled white streaks in them, exposing the white of the rods.

text: Ní an yaa bàà sá ú pà-mi puee ní ú cúú kpántí. U kpántì-mi míǹ ní áli ŋfunyɔm saalaa pu, ama Unimbɔti kaa kíí ú ŋá-mi digaŋgam.
translated: They wanted to kill me or put me in prison. They put me in prison for seven years, but God did not allow me to die there.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.

translated: The reason we are gathered here today is so that I can explain to you about how a person should live peacefully and quietly and humbly.

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb yōoǹ u-biyaamu nì u-nimpoobi ki kāaǹ igutaamu pu kí yīkì.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Niinee ní Laaban yōoǹ u-nabiyaamu ní bi nín jì Jaakɔb boon. Bi cōom̀ iwiin iluli ní ki tin nín cúú-u Galaadi jool pu.
translated: Then Laban and his relatives and Jacob ate. They spent the night there at the place called the hill of Galaad.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Ama un cee ní a yaa ká a-waaŋee, bí kpɔ̀-u. Ti-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ di bí nee. Dāan̄ m-cee kí kpèè a yaa ká a-wanti tiba á yòon̄.» Jaakɔb kaa nìn nyí yii Raseel di yùn̄ awaa.
translated: But if you come to my relatives and they refuse you, then you will be free from my oath. Only do not take my son back there." So Jacob swore an oath to him about this matter.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Tiba di yaa cúú a-waŋkuluuku kubɔee, maa cāabìń-si diwaŋkpil, see ḿ nyàab̀ kí téeǹ kun an cúúyee yil. A-waŋkuti tin bi yù ŋwiimpuee nì tin bi yù kunyeeuee, a bàlifī-mi ní ḿ téeǹ-si.
translated: When you arrive at the chief's compound, don't just enter the courtyard, wait until someone comes to call you inside. The chief's children and his wives are there, they will respect you and call you.

text: An ŋmāntì man nì si tí sòoǹ kí bóob́ kí pòon̄ tɔb, án nīn sá kudaaŋŋu man nì si kansikin.»
translated: And if a man does not have money to buy a goat, he will bring two doves or two pigeons.

translated: The prophets said that they should not be afraid. They said that their God would help them and give them strength.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.

translated: Laban called the heap of stones Jegar-Sahadutha, and Jacob called the heap of stones Galeed.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Bi gíti pú niin diyindi yii Miisipa, taapu di sá yii: <Unimbɔti ń nīn bí ki wāĺ-ti.> Di sá Laaban nìn bí yii: «Unimbɔti ń nīn bí ki wāĺ man nì si kansikin ti yaa kaa dāa gíti bí ki wāĺ tɔbee.
translated: When they had eaten there at Mizpah, they said: "May God watch between you and me." They said to Laban and him: "May God watch between me and you when we are absent one from another or when we separate from each other."

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Abraam nì Naakɔɔr Nimbɔtiu un sá bi-baa Nimbɔtiuee ń dāá sòoǹ man nì sii kansikin bɔti.» Ní Jaakɔb pūtì Unimbɔti un u-baa Isaaki nìn jáaḿ ki fàŋkée.
translated: Abraham and Nahor's Gods are witnesses and their fathers' Gods will judge between us." Then Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M pátì kí fōō tiŋan tin a ŋá-mi nee nì tibulcin tin a dàkā man a-tɔntɔnliu nee, kun puee ŋkpandɔ baba ní m nìn ŋúb́ ki dūtì Jɔɔdan buŋŋu nee. Ama díǹ pu nee, m yākatì m-nib nì m-waŋkuti akiti ali ní nee.
translated: I did not think that I would reach the land that the LORD my God was giving me and the cities that he had promised, but now I have crossed the Jordan River. But today, I have divided the land among my people and my relatives as he said.

text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «M gbāam̀-si, tùkù-mi a-yindi.» Ní u bí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a là kí béé m-yindi?» Niin niin ní u bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon.

translated: And he told them this parable: "My brothers, Esau came and asked his father saying: <Father, what will you give me and what will I inherit from your wealth?>"

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,

translated: And when you enter a house, greet them saying: "Peace be with you and may peace remain."

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.

translated: When morning came, he kissed his daughters and grandchildren goodbye, blessed them, and then left to return home. Jacob continued on his way to meet Esau

translated: And he prophesied saying: "What God has not cursed concerning Jacob, and what he has not denounced concerning Israel. It is a people that dwells alone and does not count itself among the nations, and their number is great."

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.

text: Eesayuu ká binimpoobi nì mbiyaamuee ní u bālifì Jaakɔb yii: «Binib bilab di pá a-boon nan ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «An sá mbiyaamu mun Unimbɔti ŋá man a-tɔntɔnliu tiŋan ki pú-mee.»

text: Ní Eesayuu kíí-u yii: «M-nabiki, m cáá tigaajati tikpil, dū a-wanti kí kpāafì.»

translated: The chief said to his younger brother, "Go and bring me a wife. I want to marry today. If you don't find one and bring her to me, I will kill you."

translated: On the third day, when they were still in pain, two of Jacob's sons, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, took their swords and attacked the unsuspecting city, killing every male.

translated: Jacob's sons came upon the slain and plundered the city, because their sister had been defiled.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Bi nìn cúú bi-piii nì bi-ŋoobui nì bi-naai nì bi-mammu nì tin kɔkɔ nìn pée bí kitiŋ poon nì tisatinee.
translated: They have their children and their grandchildren and their great-grandchildren and their great-great-grandchildren and other things that they left behind as inheritance.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”

translated: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi: "You have brought trouble upon me! You have made me odious among the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites and the Perizzites. My men are few in number. If they unite against me and attack me, I and my household will be destroyed."

ref: God told Jacob, “Return to Bethel, where I appeared to you when you were running from your brother Esau. Make your home there and build an altar for me.”
text: Dalbee ní Unimbɔti bí Jaakɔb yii: «Yīkì doo kí nīn jɔ́ḿ Beeteel kí cùú nīn kóò niin. Á mā nsaraafan laakin m nìn nyántì m-ba ki dàkā-si ŋyunti ŋun a nìn sáń a-maan Eesayuuyee.»
translated: Then God said to Jacob: "Get up and go to Bethel and live there. Build an altar to me who appeared to you when you were fleeing from your brother Esau."

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: Ŋyunti ŋun u bíi yáliŋkí imatiŋween ki làá màal̀ee ní umatimmaanli bí-u yii: «Pɔ́ɔǹ a-ba, kibininjabiki ní a làá gíti màal̀.»
translated: "When the man saw that he could not overpower him, he said to him: 'Let me go, for the day is breaking.'"

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Eesayuu cáá Kanaan tiŋkin ní binimpoobi. Ada un nìn sá Eelɔn, ueetija bisalee nì Ooliibama un nìn sá Ana bisal nì Siibeeyɔn ueefija naantiiuee.
translated: Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan. He married Adah daughter of Elon, a Hittite woman, and Oholibamah daughter of Anah and granddaughter of Zibeon, a Hivite woman.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Míǹ pu ní Eesayuu, un bi gíti yii Eedoomee, cūtìi kóòń Seeyiir jool pu. Eesayuu naantiibi
translated: This is about Esau, who is also called Edom, in the hill country of Seir. This is Esau's genealogy

ref: Zibeon's sons were Aiah and Anah—the same Anah who found an oasis in the desert while taking the donkeys of his father out to pasture.
text: Siibeeyɔn jipɔmbi di sá Aya nì Ana. Ana gbanti di sá un dāa nìn ká dinyimbɔbil diba kuteeun ŋyunti ŋun u nìn bíi kpàá u-baa Siibeeyɔn mammuee.
translated: The Gibeonites deceived Joshua and the Israelites. The Israelites made a covenant with them without asking God, and they became servants to the Gibeonites forever.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Diisan jipɔmbi di sá Usi nì Aran.
translated: Today we went to see Uncle and Auntie.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Ní uninjee bí-u yii: «Bi yíkì doo, di sá yaadalee m nìn gbìl̄ bi bíi lì yii: <Tí yīkìmaan doo kí nīn cá Dootan.>» Ní Jooseefi nín jì bi-boon Dootan ní ki tin ká-bi niin.
translated: And the man said to him: "They have left here, because I heard them saying: <Let us go to Dothan.>" So Joseph went after his brothers and found them in Dothan.

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Niinee ní u-mambi kíí-u yii: «Mimmee a dàkafī yii sii di làá nīn sá ti-bɔtiu deeyaaa, àá sii di làá nīn sá ti-kpiliu ní timbi ń nīn bí sii taapu ní?» U-damintiiku gbanti nì u nín sòon̄ puee yāntì bi-náŋki mɔ̄tìi wíiǹ u-pu.
translated: His mother said to him: "Why do you always go to the farm and come back empty-handed, while others go to the farm and return with loads on their heads?"

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Boonee ní u gíti dámintì ní ki tí dūu máafì u-mambi yii: «M tí dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ. M dámintì yii ŋwìiń nì uŋmal nì iŋmalbijaa saalaa nì ubɔ dòoń ki gbàantí m-nimbiin.»
translated: One day he told his wife that he was going to the farm and said: "I am going to my farm to work. My farm is very far in the bush and the wild animals there are destroying my crops."

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Ní u-baa bí-u yii: «Á cù kí kpèè yii a-mambi pɔ̄ɔ̄ɔɔ, bi-waŋkuti mun bí mana, kí gítiń kí tùkù-mi.» Eebrɔn tantakalin ní bi nìn bí ní Jooseefi baa tú-u ní u yíkìi búntì Siikeem tiŋkin u-mambi cee.
translated: They said to him: "We have come to look for our brothers, the sons of our father, who are tending the flocks." The Hebrews found them and they told Joseph's brothers that they had seen them heading toward Shechem to tend their father's flocks.

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Bi nín ká-u u páaǹní ndan kaa láá bāań-bee, ní bi sòon̄ ki bɔ́ntì yii bí kpɔ̀-u.
translated: If you don't give him money to buy food, then you should cook so that you can give him.

ref: When Reuben returned to the well and did not find Joseph there, he tore his clothes in sorrow.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun Rubeen dāa cūtì kí nyāntí Jooseefi dilɔkɔgindinee, u bàn̄ee u kpá, ní an wùn̄-u tikpil ní u dáaĺ tiwan tin u pēēyee ki cátì.
translated: When Reuben returned to the pit where Joseph had been thrown, he found it empty, so he tore his clothes in grief and returned to his brothers crying.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee Madiyan yab bíǹ dūu bàn̄ Eejipiti ní ki mun nìkī Jooseefi ki nyáfiǹ Pootifaar, ujanjakpil un cí Faaroo cindee.
translated: The Midianite merchants sold him in Egypt to a man named Joseph to Potiphar, the captain of Pharaoh's guard.

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee, Juuda yíkì u-nabiyaamu cee ki búntì Adulaam tiŋkin kijapaai kiba cee, bi yíì-u yii Yiira.
translated: One day, Judah left his brothers and went down to visit a man of Adullam whose name was Hirah.

ref: While there he met the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite man. Judah married her,
text: Niin ní Juuda ká ukanaanja Suuwa bisal ki cáá ní u nì u kpáfì.
translated: Then Judah said to Simeon his brother, "Come with me into my allotted territory."

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Ní Juuda bí Oonan yii: «Fōō a-maan nimpuu ukpiipu kii mara nín pée sá puee, kí màal̀ kí téeǹ u-yil u-dicaŋku ń taa kúntí.»
translated: Judah said to Onan: "Go in to your brother's wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her, and raise up offspring for your brother."

ref: Hirah asked the people of Enaim, “Where is the prostitute who sat along the road outside your town?” “There's never been one here,” they answered.
text: Ní u bālifì binib bin bí niinee yii: «Kinimpulaakpaai kin kàal̀ Eeniyayiim tiŋkin doo, nsan caŋinee bí la?» Ní bi kíí-u yii: «Kinimpulaakpaai kiba pée kpá doo.»
translated: And he asked the people who were standing there: "What will be done for the man who kills this Philistine?" And they told him: "The king will give him great wealth."

ref: “Those are mine!” Judah admitted. “She's a better person than I am, because I broke my promise to let her marry my son Shelah.” After this, Judah never slept with her again.
text: Juuda béè tiwanee ní ki bí yii: «U cáá ibaamɔn ki jítiǹ man. An bàà sá ḿ dū-u kí tīī m-jipɔɔn Seera, ní maa ŋá míǹ». Ní Juuda kaa ti pée gbēē-u.
translated: Judah said to his brother Simeon: "Come with me to my allotted territory so that we may fight against the Canaanites, and I will also go with you to your territory." So Simeon went with him.

ref: Tamar later gave birth to twins. But before either of them was born, one of them stuck a hand out of her womb. The woman who was helping tied a red thread around the baby's hand and explained, “This one came out first.”
text: Ŋyunti ŋun u bíi màal̀ee ní jaasibee kansikin ubɔ nyántìń u-ŋal, ní umatimmaanli dū gulfaman ki kúĺ, ní ki bí yii: «Un bí nee di péé ki nyáń.»
translated: When you see someone who is poor and you have compassion for them, and you give them something to eat, and they say: "May God bless you."

ref: Potiphar liked Joseph and made him his personal assistant, putting him in charge of his house and all of his property.
text: An puee Jooseefi nìn ká diyimɔɔl u-kpiliu cee u dū-u ki kpántì úǹ u-ba tɔntɔnliu, ní ki dū-u ki ŋá ukpil ú nīn kpèé u-cindi poon tuuŋŋi nì tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.
translated: When Joseph saw that his father had placed his right hand on Ephraim's head, he was displeased and took his father's hand to move it from Ephraim's head to Manasseh's head.

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: U-cal Pootifaar dāa bāańee ní u gíiǹ ki téeǹ míǹ mbaantiim yii: «Ueebruja uyumbu un a cáań-tee dɔ́miń kí dòon̄ nì mi.
translated: Potiphar's wife lied to her husband saying: "That Hebrew slave you brought to stay with us came to me.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ní ujanjakpilee lēē Jooseefi ú nīn bí bi-cee ki tútúǹ-bi atuun. Bi nìn yúntì sarka poon ŋwaam. Jooseefi lēē bisarkadambi bili damintiiti taapu
translated: Joseph's brothers hated him and could not speak peacefully to him. They plotted evil against him. Joseph's brothers saw him coming from afar.

ref: One night each of the two men had a dream, but their dreams had different meanings.
text: Bi bí sarkan niin ní kunyeeu kubɔee, un pūul̀ Eejipiti bɔtiu kpɔnɔbee nì un bàtī ndaam ki tìì-uee liitil dámintì, báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.
translated: When the king saw that they were not going to leave, he ordered the Egyptians to make them work harder and to give them more difficult tasks, so that they would forget about their God and his worship.

ref: The next morning, when Joseph went to see the men, he could tell they were upset,
text: Kutaa wúntèe Jooseefi dɔ́miń bi-cee ní ki ká yii an kaa mɔ̄ǹ-bi.
translated: The part of Joseph's body that they did not eat was his bones.

ref: “We each had a dream last night,” they answered, “and there is no one to tell us what they mean.” Joseph replied, “Doesn't God know the meaning of dreams? Now tell me what you dreamed.”
text: Ní bi kíí-u yii: «Ti dámintì ní ubɔ kaa bí kí tùkù-ti taapu.» Ní Jooseefi bí-bi yii: «Unimbɔti di kaa tùkù tidamintiil taapuɔɔɔ? Míǹ puee māafìmaan-mi ni nín dámintì puee.»
translated: And they said to him: "We have each dreamed a dream and there is no one to interpret it." And Joseph said to them: "Do not interpretations belong to God? Tell me your dreams."

ref: and in three days the king will pardon you. He will make you his personal servant again, and you will serve him his wine, just as you used to do.
text: An kíǹ iwiin ita Faaroo làá nyāntí-si sarkan kí gíiǹ a-tundi kí ŋúbíǹ-si, ní á tīī-u tinyɔkaal kii a nín nìn pée kpààa tìì-u puee.
translated: And when Pharaoh's daughter opened it and saw him, the child was crying and she had compassion on him and said this is one of the Hebrew children.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Ama m gbāam̀-si, bi yaa fātìi fōō-si ní a-pɔbil sɔ̄ŋkèe, a mun ń téetí kí ŋá-mi tiniti kí sòoǹ m-bɔti kí tùkù Faaroo kí nyāntí-mi sarka nee poon.
translated: But I will harden his heart so that he will not let the people go, and I will multiply my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt so that Pharaoh will know my power and glory.

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Iwiin ita dalee, an dūu ŋmāntiǹ Faaroo maaliwiiŋŋu jiŋaaliu, ní u yíiń u-tɔntɔnliibi kɔkɔ jiŋaal. U yāntì bi nyántì bin bàtī ndaam ki tìì-uee kpiliu nì bin pūul̀-u kpɔnɔbee kpiliu ki cáań u-tɔntɔnliibi kɔkɔ nimbiin.
translated: At that time, when the Israelites were slaves in Egypt, they made bricks without straw. They mixed clay with water and put it in molds and dried it in the sun to make bricks for their slave masters.

ref: Suddenly, seven fat, healthy cows came up from the river and started eating grass along the bank.
text: U yú mimmee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlee poon ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.
translated: When a person does not have good behavior, they are not respected by others, and people do not want to associate with them or listen to their advice.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: U kpēēyee ní ki ká ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee nyáń ki kútì idifin in béeyee pu.
translated: The children should not play with sharp objects because they might injure themselves.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Di sá dalbee, a nìn fōō timbi a-tɔntɔnliibi pu diŋuul ki cáá man nì bin pūul̀-si kpɔnɔbee kpiliu ki tɔ́ sarkan ujanjakpil un kpèé a-sarkadambee dumpu.
translated: In the past, he used to gather his friends to go to the farm and they would help him harvest groundnuts and give the chief a portion as tribute to the authorities.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Man nì u nìn dámintì kunyabaantiiu poon ní báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.
translated: When you are teaching children you must know your teaching and your work.

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Niinee ní Faaroo tú bi yíiń Jooseefi. Ní bi nyántì-u kusarkadiin malaa, ní u kōol̀ ki kpántì u-wampeeŋkaati ní ki nín cá ubɔti nimbiin.
translated: Pharaoh's daughter found Joseph. She took pity on the orphan child, and raised him and made him one of her own children.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: M yúee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlin ki dɔ́miń ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.
translated: When we want to prepare food, we first wash our hands, then we peel the vegetables and we cut them and put them in the pot.

ref: But you couldn't tell it, because these skinny cows were just as skinny as they were before. At once, I woke up.
text: Ní a yaa ká-iee, aa lì yii i náĺ tiba gba. I nín pée bí pu ŋgɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimu puee, míǹ ní i gbíntìi bí. Ní m cúu fīntì.
translated: When you go there, you will see a big tree. If you want to rest under its shade, then you can sit down. I will wait here.

ref: Then seven other heads of grain came up. They were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Ní ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee mun nyáń ki kútì idifin ipeekaayee pu.
translated: We should not just follow our traditions without understanding why we are doing those things.

ref: The seven skinny, ugly cows that came up later also stand for seven years, as do the seven bad heads of grain that were scorched by the desert wind. The dreams mean there will be seven years when there won't be enough grain.
text: Nnagɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimu mululi mun páaǹní ipeekaayee boonee, taapu di sá abin aluli mun. Ní ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee taapu sá ŋkùm abin aluli.
translated: If you want to do something good, don't let people know about it. And don't let your left hand know what your right hand is doing.
